title: Sunday, January 7
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
January 7, 2018 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

January 7, 2018 falls on the First Sunday after the Epiphany, also known as the Baptism of Our Lord Sunday in the liturgical calendar. The RCL readings would have been Isaiah 42:1-9 (the Servant Song), Psalm 29, Acts 10:34-43 (Peter's speech at Cornelius's house), and Matthew 3:13-17 (Jesus's baptism), focusing on themes of divine favor, the Holy Spirit's work, and Christian identity. The church was in the season after Christmas, moving from the Incarnation toward Lent, in what might be called the season of Epiphany or Ordinary Time depending on the specific tradition.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a period following the 57th General Convention (June 2015) and ahead of the 79th General Convention scheduled for Austin, Texas in July 2018. Most notably, the Church was continuing to navigate internal tensions over LGBTQ+ inclusion and biblical interpretation that had intensified significantly in recent years. The Diocese of Colorado, led by Bishop Robert O'Neill (who served 2008-2023), would have been focused on routine diocesan ministry without major reported crises or elections at this specific moment.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver in early January 2018 was experiencing typical winter weather patterns after a relatively mild holiday season. The city was in the early weeks of the 2018 Colorado legislative session (which began January 8), with attention on education funding, water rights, and other state issues. Saint John's Cathedral, located on the edge of Capitol Hill, would have been aware of ongoing discussions about the neighborhood's gentrification and changing character, though no specific major incident was reported in this week.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Trump administration was in its second year; the previous week had seen continued coverage of the investigation by Special Counsel Robert Mueller into Russian interference in the 2016 election. The government was still recovering from holiday travel disruptions and dealing with winter weather across much of the nation. The "#MeToo" movement continued to dominate cultural conversation, with new allegations against prominent figures emerging regularly; this was during the peak period of the movement's influence on American institutions and public discourse.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Winter Olympics were approximately one month away (opening February 9, 2018 in Pyeongchang, South Korea), with anticipation building around the Games and ongoing questions about Russian participation following doping scandals. Tensions remained high on the Korean Peninsula, with North Korea's weapons program a continued source of concern and diplomatic activity. The Syrian civil war was ongoing with Russia and Iran continuing their military support for Assad's government against various opposition forces.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

Early 2018 reflected a nation still processing significant cultural and political divisions following 2016. The dominant mood was one of cultural reckoning (via #MeToo), political polarization, and uncertainty about institutional stability. In Denver specifically, the Broncos had just completed a disappointing 5-11 NFL season, and local attention was turning to the NHL's Colorado Avalanche and NBA's Denver Nuggets. The broader cultural zeitgeist emphasized questions of power, accountability, and identity—themes that might have resonated differently depending on the cathedral's pastoral concerns and the particular focus of Rev. Lawson's sermon.
